source_document.type: NBA official scorer report / gamebook PDF

halftime_state.read_summary: Cleveland trailed by 4 despite poor 2P efficiency, heavy 3PA volume, and strong free throw access. The live read favored Cleveland second-half correction rather than Detroit continuation.

non_logged_or_secondary_market_notes.diagnostic_note: Would have missed if played; Detroit 2H scoring cooled to 47 after 56-point first half. Do not assume first-half DET efficiency carries when Cleveland defensive pressure and Mitchell scoring takeover are live.

model_result.closing_score_support: Cleveland reached 90 through 3Q and finished 112 despite only 22 in Q4.

first_quarter.read: Detroit survived an early Cleveland 11-point lead and flipped the quarter through LeVert shot-making and Cleveland cold half-court shooting.
first_quarter.model_note: Do not overrate early Cleveland control if Donovan Mitchell is cold, but track free throw access and turnover creation as correction signals.

second_quarter.read: Detroit kept offensive efficiency high and entered halftime up 56-52. Cleveland stayed close through threes and free throws despite weak interior scoring.
second_quarter.model_note: Halftime live edge came from Cleveland's points being suppressed by poor FG% rather than lack of creation.

third_quarter.read: Cleveland's game flipped immediately after halftime. Donovan Mitchell produced a takeover quarter and Detroit's primary creation collapsed under turnover pressure.
third_quarter.model_note: This is the decisive validation point for the CLE 106.5 ghost over. Cleveland scored 38 in Q3, moving from 52 to 90 before Q4.

fourth_quarter.read: Cleveland protected a double-digit lead through foul-line scoring and late Mitchell usage. Detroit made a small late push but never fully threatened the team-total result.
fourth_quarter.model_note: The CLE over remained safe because Q3 banked enough points before late-game pace softened.

cleveland.donovan_mitchell: 43 points, 13-26 FG, 4-12 3P, 13-15 FT; 39 second-half points after 4 first-half points.
cleveland.james_harden: 24 points and 11 assists; playmaking stabilized Cleveland's half-court shot creation.
cleveland.evan_mobley: 17 points, 8 rebounds, 5 assists, 5 blocks; defensive spine and second-half rim deterrence.
cleveland.free_throw_edge: Cleveland finished 30-34 FT while Detroit was only 9-12 FT.

detroit.caris_levert: 24 points on 10-16 shooting; carried Detroit through first-half bench offense.
detroit.tobias_harris: 16 points and 8 rebounds, but scored only 2 second-half points after 14 at halftime.
detroit.cade_cunningham: 19 points, 6 assists, 5 turnovers; pressure and second-half turnover issues limited Detroit's ceiling.
detroit.turnover_problem: Detroit finished with 20 turnovers leading to 25 Cleveland points.

why_the_ghost_bet_hit: Cleveland had strong free throw access at halftime and finished with a major FT volume edge.
Halftime scoring was being held down by poor FG% rather than lack of offensive process.
Detroit's turnover profile created second-half live-ball and short-clock opportunities for Cleveland.
Mitchell's first-half cold shooting created a positive regression/takeover path rather than a fade signal.
Cleveland's 38-point third quarter created enough buffer to survive a slower 22-point fourth quarter.
what_the_model_got_right: Correctly treated the first-half score as unstable because Cleveland's underlying creation and FT profile were stronger than the raw 52 points implied.
Correctly targeted the team total rather than spread or moneyline, avoiding margin volatility while capturing Cleveland scoring correction.
Correctly kept the ticket as ghost-sized due to bankroll reset mode.
remaining_risk_flags: Cleveland scoring was heavily dependent on Mitchell's takeover and free throws; this is powerful but volatile.
Detroit's late mini-run shows that Cleveland spread/margin markets still carry backdoor risk.
Second-half overs should still require process confirmation, not just halftime deficit chasing.

patch_rules.rule: At halftime, separate poor shooting from poor creation. If a team has strong 3PA volume, FT access, and assist/paint creation but trails or underperforms raw points, allow team-total over consideration even when FG% is poor.
patch_rules.trigger_from_game: CLE had 52 at half on 39.5% FG but had 25 3PA, 16 FTA, and 12 assists.

patch_rules.rule: For elite high-usage playoff scorers, a cold first half with normal minutes and shot volume should not automatically suppress second-half team total. Add takeover tail when team remains within 8 points and usage remains high.
patch_rules.trigger_from_game: Mitchell had 4 first-half points on 1-8 FG, then scored 39 in the second half.

patch_rules.rule: Downgrade Detroit second-half team-total overs when turnovers reach 10+ by halftime or Cunningham/Ausar/Duren combine for high live-ball turnover pressure, especially against switchable rim-protection teams.
patch_rules.trigger_from_game: Detroit had 10 halftime turnovers and 20 final turnovers, producing 25 Cleveland points off turnovers.

patch_rules.rule: When a halftime team-total over target gets 32+ points in Q3, mark position as protected and do not overreact to Q4 pace suppression unless the remaining required points exceed 18.
patch_rules.trigger_from_game: Cleveland scored 38 in Q3, reaching 90 through three quarters.

rolling_window_use: High-weight Game 5 input. The series is now tied 2-2. Cleveland's second-half correction profile, Mitchell takeover tail, free throw access, and Detroit turnover pressure should be elevated. Detroit first-half efficiency must be discounted if turnover pressure remains high. Cleveland team-total overs can be playable when halftime process metrics are stronger than raw score, but spread/margin plays remain vulnerable to backdoor scoring.
